audit_summary.summary_text: Static review of the provided skill content found a HIGH severity SC2 pattern: the documentation instructs users and CI pipelines to execute a remote install script via `curl ... | bash`. No other malicious prompt-injection, exfiltration, credential-access, or hidden-instruction patterns were present in the provided file. The dangerous behavior is openly documented rather than hidden, so this appears intent-aligned but unsafe.

vulnerabilities.title: Remote Script Fetching via `curl | bash`
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The skill instructs operators to download and immediately execute a shell script from an external domain (`https://depcheck-engine.com/install.sh`) by piping it directly into `bash`. This is a classic remote-code-execution anti-pattern because the fetched content is not pinned, checksum-verified, signature-verified, or reviewed before execution. The same unsafe pattern is repeated in the CI example, which would run arbitrary code inside an automated build environment if the remote endpoint, DNS resolution, TLS termination, or distribution infrastructure were ever compromised.
vulnerabilities.code_evidence:
curl -fsSL https://depcheck-engine.com/install.sh | bash
...
- run: curl -fsSL https://depcheck-engine.com/install.sh | bash
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: Anyone following the documented setup or CI instructions could execute attacker-controlled code on their workstation or CI runner. In CI, this could expose repository contents, build secrets, tokens, and supply-chain integrity. Because the script is executed directly from the network, compromise of the hosting domain or delivery path would immediately translate into code execution.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Replace `curl | bash` installation with a safer distribution mechanism: publish versioned packages through trusted package managers, or require downloading a specific release artifact with a pinned checksum/signature and executing it only after verification. For CI, use a pinned, reviewed action or a vendored installer committed to the repository, and document integrity verification steps explicitly.
